@stephenbarrette610
replies.text:
@jackgraham3 Thanks for the compliment! I’m not particularly smart (but luckily at my advanced years I can still stay awake most days - unlike some politicians my age …) 

I completely agree with you that when TOS aired it did cause some controversy over its handling of social issues and in particular the first ‘inter-racial’ kiss shown on network television. 

But maybe you missed the slightly nuanced point I was making. Like all good science fiction Star Trek has told stories that can be taken as allegories of contemporary issues, but without having to scream these out loud. It’s a personal opinion but I think by pursuing this overt, virtual signalling you can end up doing a disservice to the very causes you are supposedly supporting. This is the red meat for certain commentators to shout ‘woke - DEI’ and does not help anyone.

Also (and once again a personal view) a number of the current iterations of Star Trek seem to play fast and loose with the lore of the show and its wealth of content. 

Take a tiny instance, the use of contemporary sayings, slang and swear words. For example ‘pissed’ (from the late 14th century ‘pissier’) was first used as ‘unhappy about something’ in the mid-20th century and would unlikely to be used by teens in the 32nd century, ~3195, over 1,100 years from now. 

The same could be said for other vulgarities uttered by the cast. And just because you CAN swear on streaming, it doesn’t mean you HAVE to and indeed comes as a shock when characters on popular shows switch - (Damages, The Good Fight et al.)  As LeVar Burton said on a podcast recently the strict syntax of Star Trek was very deliberate and precise and at times almost Shakespearean. Imagine if Gene Roddenberry had allowed his writers to use 1960’s contemporary slang? - ‘Right on Kirk’, ‘That’s far out Spock’, ‘Uhura is one groovy chick.’ It would have sounded stupid by the mid 1970s …

It’s this sloppy, careless and scientifically stupid writing I’m not particularly impressed with. Another example is that almost every character in Academy has an "issue.’ Then there’s the excessive LED’s on every part of the set that make it look like a 70’s night club, or the choice of a backlight glare or flare in most two shot conversations (presumably to make the dialogue more ‘urgent’?) 

I’ll skip the bad and not particularly engaging stories as I’m far too old to enjoy a teen action drama, but to quote others, this could be ‘Saved by the Bell IN SPACE! (And don’t even start me on warp drives spontaneously blowing up across the entire galaxy … because an angst ridden teen screamed a bit too loudly?) 

Finally just dumb stuff such as in a thousand years time we apparently haven’t cured shortsightedness and lost the formula for semaglutide (Ozempic) …


[Starfleet Academy had by episode three already fallen out of the Paramount+ ratings being beaten by Sponge Bob, Everyone Loves Raymond and Buffy reruns. A sad situation for a reported $100 million show.]

replies.author: @Viral9
replies.text: @tidefan35967 Roddenberry's vision of the future was one where humanity had settled its differences, certainly, but why is that a progressive (ie. political) notion? A good step toward settling our differences might be to stop pointing them out all the time. Identifying as nothing more than a member of the human species would be a huge improvement over the tribalism and self-righteousness associated with politics and social activism today. Starfleet Academy was a _military_ academy. Its mission was never activism or advocating for social change, it was about upholding the ideals of the Federation, leaving others to their own devices, to explore and better understand the universe we're apart of, and provide the opportunity for personal pursuits, unless and until any pursuits negatively impacted others. If they no longer have the Prime Directive in this new schlocky farther flung future, fine, but it's very clear Roddenberry would not be a fan of the dark, depressing, and farcical places Trek has gone in the last couple decades.

author: @Ncyphen
text:
My biggest complaint is constantly hear people go, "Well, Star Trek has always been 'woke' and progressive."  Yes, Star Trek has always been about differences in people and learning to live and love one another while living in a communal society; however, the show made sure to establish it's lore and live by the lore.

In order to create a living and thriving universe for a story, you must establish rules that the world lives by, but once you start breaking those rules, the immersiveness and believability of the world starts to fall apart.  Something that was established in the earlier movies/series was that individuals at Starfleet had to be physically fit, which matches the same ideologies of real naval academies.  The training is strict, just like real life.  Why is everyone so aloof, relaxed, and out of shape?  If there's ever danger on a starship vessel, you'd want your crew to be fit and physical enough to be able to weather any situation.

The immersion is broken and the world no longer makes any sense.  The show runners should have sent writers and people to an actual naval academy to observe how students are taught and live then use that experience to shape and build their show.  It's clear that the intent of this show wasn't to create a believe able show, but rather Paramount wanted a fun college based show set in the Star Trek universe.

It's really sad how both Star Trek and Star Wars have been ruined by executive greed seeking to push political agendas.  Of course, when the shows/movies fail, their cast and crew all complain about how "right wing" influencers ruined the possibility of the show/movie to succeed by spreading lies and talking trash about their property.

author: @JamesR624
text:
2:29 Fun fact, the iPad is actually named after the thing it was inspired by; Star Trek's "PADDs". Steve was a huge Star Trek fan. It was a combination of that acronym with Apple's "i for internet" naming convention. In essence, the "iPad" name is actually an acronym.
PADD: Personal Access Display Device.
iPad: Internat Personal Access Device.

So yes, both the "Cell Phone" and the "Tablet" as we know them today, came from Star Trek; in the forms of the Motorola DynaTak cellular phone, and the Apple iPad tablet.

author: @AlexIsModded
text:
The design of the Starship Athena makes perfect sense, because the earliest depictions of the goddess in Greek art, show her with wings. They did their homework in that regard. It's also smart to choose something that not many people today are familiar with, when creating an entity that exists 800 years after TOS.

Even though I've been a trekkie since I was a little kid (grew up watching Next Gen, just to age myself a bit), a gripe I've always had is that for a franchise about inclusivity, the alien races are much too "perfectly pure". Other than Spock who was half human, and the Borg which are a blend of everybody, that was as far as they went with the hybrids. So far in the show (if you don't watch Discovery) we don't know what the Burn really was, but we do know it was some type of cataclysmic event. We also know in modern day that when a species' population starts to dwindle, their breeding becomes desperate. Take the cheetah for example, they are breeding genetic weaknesses now due to low numbers in the wild. So why wouldn't groups of alien races breed in order to preserve some semblance of who/what they are? Better to exist as two parts of something, than to not exist at all.

Maybe this is what the higher order type of message will be for this series going forward.

replies.author: @siral2000
replies.text: Browser extensions. They are based off of the dislikes of the people that have the extension. Not sure how it's calculated so depending on the extension the number might be a projection based on the people that have the extension. (All views / Extension views * Extension dislikes=Projected Dislikes) So the number could be bloated or low. Since those who use the extension fall into a "type" of people and not a true average of users. The extension might also be a straight count which would definitely be a low number.

I loved star trek growing up. I gave up completely on nutrek after that horrific 3rd movie where amongst other things they destroyed the enemy with beastie boys music. (I mean WTF?) It's sad that most fans are more hoping that the franchise will just be allowed to rest in peace, rather than any kind of enthusiasm for new projects.

author: @adrianjenkins5877
text:
Watching this video reminded me of my single favorite Star Trek episode ever ("The Balance of Terror"). In that episode,  Lt. Stiles was clearly prejudiced against Spock due to his appearance,. Obviously, it shown at the end that Spock is no traitor, and Stiles affirms and thanks him for his life-saving efforts. Kirk clearly supports Spock throughout the episode, yet he still engages his junior officer, going so far as to affirm his request for opinions in their sitdown. A great episode, it at once tackles prejudice, but never stoops to becoming an after-school special on inclusivity.

Can you imagine how such an episode would be written today? Do you even want to?
